coredata.dc:title: Correction pen as a hydrophobic/lipophobic barrier plotter integrated with paper-based chips and a mini UV-torch to implement all-in-one device for determination of carbazochrome
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shaheny, Rania
Al-Khateeb, Lateefa A.
El Hamd, Mohamed A.
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: The design of a cheap, simple, and handy sensing system for rapid quantitation of pharmaceuticals becomes mandatory to ease drug development procedures, quality control, health care, etc. This work describes a simple, innovative, and easily manufactured paper-based device using a correction pen as a plotter for hydrophobic/lipophobic barriers and graphene quantum dots for recognition and quantification of the hemostatic drug carbazochrome, via fluorescence turn-off mechanism mediated by the inner filter effect. A smartphone-based all-in-one device fitted with an inexpensive 365 nm flashlight as a UV light source and a free image processing software was developed for rapid and reliable interpretation of the fluorescence change from the paper-based device upon introduction of the drug. The simple and convenient steps permit the analysis of many samples in a very short time. The smartphone-based all-in-one device featured excellent sensitivity for carbazochrome with a limit of detection equals to 12 ng/detection zone and good %recovery (100.0 ± 0.4). The reliability of the device was ascertained by favorable statistical comparison with the analogous optimized conventional fluorimetry method and a reference HPLC method. The device has been successfully applied for versatile quantitation of carbazochrome in tablets and on manufacturing equipment surfaces with excellent recoveries. The device offers many green aspects that definitely assist the implementation of the sustainability concept to analytical laboratories. The cost-efficiency, reliability, and ease of fabrication as well as the greenness and user friendship qualify the device for wide application in low-income communities.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of a cheap, simple, and handy sensing system for rapid quantitation of pharmaceuticals becomes mandatory to ease drug development procedures, quality control, health care, etc. This work describes a simple, innovative, and easily manufactured paper-based device using a correction pen as a plotter for hydrophobic/lipophobic barriers and graphene quantum dots for recognition and quantification of the hemostatic drug carbazochrome, via fluorescence turn-off mechanism mediated by the inner filter effect. A smartphone-based all-in-one device fitted with an inexpensive 365 nm flashlight as a UV light source and a free image processing software was developed for rapid and reliable interpretation of the fluorescence change from the paper-based device upon introduction of the drug. The simple and convenient steps permit the analysis of many samples in a very short time. The smartphone-based all-in-one device featured excellent sensitivity for carbazochrome with a limit of detection equals to 12 ng/detection zone and good %recovery (100.0 ± 0.4). The reliability of the device was ascertained by favorable statistical comparison with the analogous optimized conventional fluorimetry method and a reference HPLC method. The device has been successfully applied for versatile quantitation of carbazochrome in tablets and on manufacturing equipment surfaces with excellent recoveries. The device offers many green aspects that definitely assist the implementation of the sustainability concept to analytical laboratories. The cost-efficiency, reliability, and ease of fabrication as well as the greenness and user friendship qualify the device for wide application in low-income communities.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, scientific research has been attempting to propose effective and innovative approaches to substitute or support classical analytical techniques, intending to ease drug development procedures. In this context, paper-based analytical devices have attracted much attention in recent years as a result of their intrinsic merits such as cost-effectiveness, ease of use and handling, portability, simplicity, feasibility of scaling-down sample and reagent volumes, and safe disposability [1]. The paper platforms are well-suited for large-scale manufacturing and pliable to long-term storage in a small area. Additionally, they allow fast analysis with no need for high skills or great experience [2]. Besides, the paper platforms show many merits compared to chip-based devices made of glass, silicon, or polymers. The chip-based devices require sophisticated fabrication procedures. Meanwhile, the disposal of paper-based devices is very simple via incineration in contrary to the chip-based devices [3].

Furthermore, with the advances in nanoscience, the modification of the paper-based devices with a nanomaterial that possesses unique optical properties has brought great progress and improvement of its design and performance as analytical tools in combination with colorimetric or fluorescence detection. Many types of nanomaterials have been applied in paper-based sensors such as metal nanoparticles, carbonaceous nanoparticles, nanocomposites, and nanohybrids [4–10].

Thanks to the great growth in the usage and coverage of smartphones all over the world, smartphone cameras have emerged as a cheap detection method for quantitative analysis in paper-based device techniques. Smartphones are handy, lightweight, do not need a skilled operator [11]. Thus, smartphone has found many recent applications as a portable detector that can substitute large instruments such as spectrometers and LC [12]. In this vein, the enormous developments in smartphones such as complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor image sensor, strong imaging hardware, and freely accessed operating systems [13] in addition to the availability of free smartphone-powered image processing software motivated us to build an all-in-one device that implements nanomaterial-modified paper-based analytical device, custom-designed sensing unit, smartphone, and free image processing software. For this purpose, we designed blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based paper-based chips for the determination of the antihemorrhagic drug carbazochrome (adrenochrome monosemicarbazone or 3-hydroxy-1-methyl-5,6-indolinedione semicarbazone, CZ). Yet, we faced a big challenge to create hydrophobic/lipophobic barriers compatible with the organic solvent-rich medium (60%ethanol) needed to dissolve the poorly water-soluble CZ [14]. It was a crucial step to pattern barriers with excellent confinement ability to this organic solvent mixture for designing the detection zones in the paper-based chips. The common hydrophobic materials used for this goal such as wax, polymers, silicone, and methylsilsequioxanes are unable to confine organic solvents which restricts their application [15]. Here, we proposed an innovative approach of using correction fluid to generate hydrophobic/lipophobic barriers for fabricating a paper-based device. This approach has many advantages compared to other techniques where it is capable of confining organic solvent, it is a very cheap and rapid technique, and it does not necessitate experience or a special device like wax printer, oven, vapor phase deposition, or flexography printing [16]. These merits permit the wide applicability of this technique, especially in resource-limited laboratories.

The distinctive luminescence properties of GQDs together with biocompatibility, and excellent sensing capability promote their incorporation in various sensing probes [13,17,18]. The applications of GQDs include also environmental sensing [17,19], bioimaging, biosensing, and therapeutic applications, e.g., drug delivery, gene delivery, and photodynamic therapy. Another advantage that distinguishes GQDs is the elimination of the toxic apprehensions linked to metal nanoparticles since GQDs are composed entirely of carbon [20]. Thus, we utilized GQDs for designing the paper-based chips for the quantification of CZ based on our initial experiments that showed the capability of CZ to efficiently quench the GQDs intense fluorescence. The smartphone-based all-in-one device was built using simple and available components with no need for distinct specifications. Fig. 1 illustrates the components of the device. An imaging dark carton box was fitted with a smartphone adaptor that is usually supplied with the phone package and a holder for the optical element. A cheap and commercially available 365 nm UV flashlight was used as the optical element. Free image processing software programs are installed from Google Apps for interpreting the taken image into an analytical signal (Fig. 1). The simple design of the device and the utility of the smartphone for controlling, monitoring, and analyzing afford cost-effectiveness and ease of handling.

Meanwhile, we conducted a complementary study using the conventional fluorimetric technique for a detailed evaluation of the mechanism of fluorescence quenching as well as for comparing the performance of the newly designed all-in-one device.

This integrated all-in-one device was applied for the determination of CZ. CZ has adrenochrome-like activity since it decreases the typical bleeding time through direct action on capillaries [21]. It is administered during and after surgical operations to decrease blood loss. As well, CZ is used for the treatment of uncomplicated hemorrhoids [22]. Recently, it has been recommended with vitamin C, vitamin E, and lysozyme for the treatment of chronic periodontitis after root planing [23]. Also, CZ shows a potential helpful action in treating hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia [24]. However, the analytical literature of CZ suffers from some limitations and shortcomings. Notably, the current fluorometric methods for CZ are very limited and classically based on ion-pair complexation with either fluorescent organic dyes [25] that suffer from poor water solubility and poor photostability [26], or amino acids [27] that are associated with some known drawbacks such as absorption/emission in the UV-region and weak photostability [28]. Furthermore, these methods [25,27] need time for complex formation between CZ and fluorophores (10 and 15 min/sample, respectively). In addition, LC/MS [29,30], TLC-densitometry, and HPLC-UV [31] methods were also reported for determination of CZ. These methods are dependent on large expensive apparatus and consume plenty of solvents and chemicals. Hence, we intended to develop an all-in-one device for the determination of CZ offering many distinctive advantages including the dependence on a simply assembled built-in device that integrates all analytical steps, no need for costly instruments, using minimal amounts of reagents and samples, and suitability for limited-resources laboratories and developing countries. Then, we tested the reliability of the developed all-in-one device for the determination of CZ active pharmaceutical ingredient in formulation and determination of its residuals on manufacturing equipment surfaces. These applications are very vital for pharmaceutical manufacturing of this drug.

A pure form of CZ was generously obtained as a gift from Minapharm Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt). GQDs suspension (1 mg/mL) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Milli-Q water obtained by Merck Millipore Milli-Q® integral water purification system (Darmstadt, Germany) was used in the entire study. CZ tablets (claimed to contain 30 mg CZ sodium sulfonate) were a product of Nichi-Iko Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Whatman #2 filter paper was used. Correction pens (products of Pony Co., LTD, China (P1), Display Can Do Co., China (P2), and Zebra Co., LTD, Japan (P3)) were purchased from local stationery. Standard solution of CZ (100 μg/mL) was prepared by dissolving 10 mg in ethanol: water (6:4, v/v) and sonication. A 100 μg/mL solution of GQDs was prepared in water.

Jasco FP-6600 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used for conventional fluorimetry experiments using a quartz cell (1 cm), at a sensitivity of 750 V, scan speed 500 nm/min, and slit widths of 5/10 nm for excitation/emission, respectively. The UV-VIS absorption spectra were recorded using Jasco V-6600 double-beam spectrophotometer and the FT/IR spectra were scanned by a Jasco FT/IR-4200 spectrophotometer. The Zeta potential of GQDs was calculated using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Ltd., Worcestershire, UK). A mini portable and USB rechargeable SV003 3 W, 365 nm UV flashlight equipped with a tightly reinforced black filter optical lens to filter out ambient light was purchased from Alonefire Co., China via the Amazon E-commerce company.

The pattern of the detection zones was designed with Microsoft PowerPoint and printed on A4-sized paper to be used as a template inserted below the Whatman filter paper. The correction pen was gently pressed to dispense the fluid on the Whatman filter paper to draw the designed pattern. The filter paper was allowed to completely dry at room temperature. Each circle has an inner diameter of 8 mm and an outer diameter of 12 mm, the thickness of the barrier equals 2 mm. GQDs solution (15 μL of 100 μg/mL) was dropped in each detection zone, after drying at room temperature (≈10 min), the paper chips were stored in a plastic container till usage. A cover consisted of a circles' pattern-perforated black origami paper was prepared using a 6 mm punch (Daiso Industries Co., LTD., Hiroshima, Japan) to cover the paper-based chip (Fig. S1, Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the smartphone-based all-in-one device
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 1, the designed device consisted of three main parts; (i) imaging box: dark carton box to eliminate the interference from ambient light adjusted with two holes for the smartphone adapter and the light source and a small tray was fixed on the floor of the box to be a settled position for the paper-based chip. The paper-based chip is covered with a 6 mm diameter circles pattern-perforated black origami paper in the same alignment as the paper-based chip, (ii) smartphone adapter fixed on the top side of the dark box to fit Samsung Galaxy S9 smartphone (Japan) that's equipped with a 12-megapixel rear camera to capture the images from fixed distance and position, and (iii) the optical element: 365 nm UV flashlight tightly fixed on the imaging box. Free image processing software (RGB Color Detector, version 1.1.4, and Color Grab, version 3.7.7.) were freely downloaded from Google Play Store and ImageJ software (National Institute of Health, Maryland, USA) was freely downloaded at (https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/download.html).

The images were analyzed following the next specified workflow:
(i)
Using the RGB color detector and Color Grab smartphone software:
(a)
The software generates color values for the area of interest selected by tapping the screen with a rectangular aperture.
(b)
Using the RGB color space: The red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values for the selected area of each detection zone were extracted, the G values were used as the analytical signal.
(c)
Using the HSV color space: The hue (H), saturation (S), and value (V) measurements were extracted for the selected area of each detection zone, the H values were used as the analytical signal.
(ii)
Using ImageJ software:
(a)
The images' RGB values were extracted and then the images were split into R, G, and B channels.
(b)
The green values (G) were used as the analytical signal.
(c)
Alternatively, the grayscale value (Y) of the green channel was obtained and used as the analytical signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for calibration curve using the paper-based device
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of 20 μL of CZ standard solution with different concentrations were dropped into the indicator zones of the paper chip using a micropipette (0.04, 0.2, 0.4, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 μg/zone). After drying at room temperature, the paper chip was covered with the circles' pattern-perforated black origami paper and fixed with adhesive so that the perforated circles are perfectly fitted on the detection zones, and placed on the tray inside the imaging box. The optical element was turned on and the images were captured by the smartphone. The experiment was done in triplicate and each image was captured three times. The images were processed as described earlier and the calibration curve of the H values versus concentrations (μg/zone) was plotted.

Ten tablets were weighed and finely triturated into a fine powder. An amount of the powder containing 50 mg of CZ was weighed and transferred into a 50 mL calibrated flask, dissolved in ethanol: water (6:4, v/v), and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was filtered to obtain a clear tablet extract. Different volumes of the obtained solution were accurately measured and transferred into a set of 10 mL calibrated flasks. The same procedure described for the construction of the calibration curve was followed and the %recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determination of drug residuals on equipment surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: Stainless-steel plates (5 × 5 cm) were rinsed with ethanol and left to dry then sprayed separately with the drug solution (10, 20, and 40 μL of 500 μg/mL solution), thus, the final deposited amounts were 5.0, 10.0, 20.0 μg CZ. The solvent was allowed to dry at room temperature. The heads of cotton swabs were rinsed with ethanol and allowed to dry at room temperature. The swabs were soaked in ethanol then used to wipe the entire surface of the stainless-steel plates vertically then horizontally starting from outside to inside. The head of the swab was cut and transferred to a small clean beaker containing 1.5 mL ethanol. The beaker was covered with a plastic wrap and sonicated for 10 min then the swab head was squeezed and removed. The solution was made up to 2.5 mL with water. The solution was mixed and filtered through 0.45 μm PTFE Syringe Filters. A control experiment was performed simultaneously by spiking a stainless-steel plate with ethanol instead of the drug solution. Twenty μL of each solution was dropped into the detection zones of the paper-based chip, allowed to dry, and transferred to the imaging box. After capturing the images, they were processed as described in Section “2.5”, and the %recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the conventional fluorimetry method
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of GQDs solution (50 μL, 100 μg/mL) were mixed with increasing concentrations of CZ in a series of 10 mL calibrated flasks. The final volumes were completed with water and the solutions were thoroughly mixed. The fluorescence spectra of the solutions were recorded in the range of 360–600 nm after excitation at 350 nm and the fluorescence intensities were measured at λ em = 440 nm (F). The fluorescence intensity of GQD without the addition of the drugs (F0) was measured simultaneously. The calibration plot of Ln (F0/F) versus drug concentration [μM] was built and the regression equation was derived.

Different spectroscopy techniques have been adopted to characterize the surface structure and the spectral properties of GQDs. The FT/IR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 2 a) shows a broad strong peak at 3367 cm−1 that is assigned to a stretch vibration of a hydroxyl group (-OH) and two peaks at 1064 and 1393 cm−1 that are assigned to C-OH stretching and O-H bending vibrations, respectively, of a hydroxyl group. This result suggested that the GQDs have surface hydroxyl groups which improve its hydrophilicity and explicate its aqueous solubility and stability. Additionally, the peak at 1250 referred to a stretch vibration of C-O of alkyl aryl ether. Meanwhile, the peak at 1633 cm−1 ascribed stretch vibration of CC of the conjugated aromatic system of the GQDs core. The peaks at 2900 and 2970 cm−1 are attributed to the stretch vibration of C-H of alkane [32].

The Zeta potential of GQDs was found to be equals to −22 ± 3.46 mv (Fig. 2b), where the negative charge is due to the hydroxyl groups on the surface. These surface negative charges contribute to maintaining the stability of GQDs solution by repulsive forces that prevent their aggregation.

The GQDs displayed a strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm upon excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 2c). The luminescence of GQDs can be described by two conceivable mechanisms: first is the core emission due to the conjugated double bonds domain of the core, and second is the surface-emission because of the hydroxyl groups attached to the graphitic core. The emission maximum (λ em) of GQDs was not influenced by the variation of the excitation wavelength λ ex from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 2d). Such performance is due to the small particle size distribution with a less defeated surface, where the response from GQDs with the same size and composition will yield the same emission spectra and vice versa [20]. The size and morphology of the GQDs have been confirmed by the TEM image and particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 2e). The GQDs have a spherical shape with an average size of 2.41 ± 0.79 nm.

The fluorescence of GQDs was dependent on the pH of the solution, displaying the best emission over the pH range of 5.3–9.0 (Fig. 2f). A large decrease in the fluorescence was apparent in acidic solution since the hydroxyl groups tend to be unionized causing a decrease of the negative charges and agglomeration of the GQDs with a subsequent decrease of its fluorescence. Conversely, at higher pH (5.3–9.0), the hydroxyl groups exist as ionized form (-O-) imparting considerable surface negative charges that kept the stability and fluorescence intensity of the GQDs [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the paper-based chip platform
ce:section.ce:para: The poor aqueous solubility of CZ mandates the utilization of an organic solvent or an organic solvent-rich mixture to enhance its solubility [14]. Therefore, in this study, we used an ethanol-water mixture (6:4, v/v) as a solvent for the preparation of CZ solutions. Thus, it was mandatory to create a lipophobic/hydrophobic barrier with excellent confinement ability to this organic solvent-rich mixture for designing the paper chip device. Meanwhile, a major drawback of wax, silicone, and methylsilsequioxanes-derived barriers is the inability to properly confine alcohols and vulnerability to dissolution, solvent diffusion, or swelling if challenged with organic solvents [15]. Thus, in this study, we proposed the first application of a correction pen for patterning hydrophobic/lipophobic barrier to design a paper-based device.

The correction fluid mostly contains titanium dioxide, resin, coloring agent, and solvent, however, some variations may exist between manufacturers. The confining capability of correction pens from different manufacturing companies (P1, P2, and P3) was studied using rhodamine 6G dye solution in 60% ethanol as a test droplet to ease rapid visualization. The three correction pens show comparable confinement capability by complete retaining of the test sample when deposited on the two sides of the filter paper (Fig. 3 a). On the other hand, it is obvious from Fig. 3b that the correction pen P1 exhibited superior confining ability when deposited on one side only while P2 and P3 failed to function properly demonstrating a significant leakage. Multiple repetitions of this experiment (n = 6) gave the same result which confirms that the leakage is attributed to improper penetration of the correction fluid from P2 and P3 into the paper and incomplete blockage of the pores. Thus, the correction pen P1 was selected in this study for plotting the barriers in further experiments offering advantages of high confining capability by deposition on one side of the paper and rapid fabrication.

In addition, two types of filter paper, Whatman #2 and Advantec #2 have been compared as substrates for the fabrication of the paper-based device. No significant difference was observed in the performance of the two types of filter papers where the patterns plotted on both substrates showed similar confinement capacity (Fig. 3c & d). This result supports the reliability of the designed platform for diverse applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the all-in-one device and image processing
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain images with high quality and reproducible results, a dark box fitted with smartphone adapter and optical element holder was assembled to avoid ambient light interference and to guarantee the correct positioning of the paper-based chips in the same place in all experiments. The dark box is a strong carton box lined with black origami paper. The smartphone adapter is a part of the phone package provided by smartphone manufacturers. The light source is a simple portable and USB rechargeable 365 nm UV flashlight equipped with a tightly reinforced black filter optical lens to filter out ambient light. The rechargeable nature of the used flashlight via USB excludes any need for a power source during analysis and corroborates the portability of the device. Furthermore, its cheapness and availability offer feasibility of using this device in any laboratories with limited resources. Fig. 4 a illustrates the image of the paper-based chip for CZ determination captured using the assembled all-in-one device.

Different image processing software was investigated for analyzing the images to obtain the quantitative indicators. Two smartphone-powered programs, “Color Grab” and “RGB Detector”, in addition to the ImageJ software run on a laptop were investigated. The three software were used to get the RGB values in the RGB color space. The RBG color space describes the color as a percentage of three mixed individual channels: red (R), green (G), and blue (B). Attempts to correlate the R, G, or B coordinates to the drug concentration revealed that the G coordinate produced a highly linear response for quantitative CZ determination by plotting (G0/G)-1 versus CZ concentration (μg/zone), where G0 is the intensity of the green channel for the blank zone and G is the intensity of green channel for the sample zone (Fig. 4b, c, & 4d). On the other hand, the calibration curves were nonlinear using the B or R coordinates.

Furthermore, the images were processed by ImageJ to get the grayscale for the green channel. In this scale, a gray value of 255 denotes white or no color while a gray value of 0 denotes a black or a darker color. The grayscale value (Y) of the green channel decreased with the increase of CZ concentration (Fig. 4a). Good linearity was also achieved by plotting (Y 0/Y)-1 versus CZ concentration (μg/zone), where Y0 is the grayscale value of the blank zone and G is the grayscale value of the sample zone (Fig. 4e).

Moreover, the performance of the paper-based device was studied using the HSV color space for quantitative measurement with “Color Grab” and “RGB Detector” software via smartphone. The HSV color model defines the color space by three coordinates: Hue (H) which means the color type (e.g. green, blue, red, magenta, etc), Saturation (S) which means the intensity of the hue, and Value (V) that represents the color brightness. An excellent linear correlation between the H value and the drug concentration was evident. Since the H value increases with the increase of drug concentration, it was feasible to directly generate the calibration plot of H value versus CZ concentration (μg/zone) without data curation (Fig. 4f & g). No linearity could be obtained by plotting either the S or the V values versus drug concentration. A summary of the linearity data is illustrated in Table 1 .

To endorse the efficiency of the different software utilized for digital image processing, we statistically compared the obtained results. The Brown-Forsythe test and Bartlett's test [34] were applied to evaluate the variance equality between the results obtained using the Color Grab, RGB Detector, and ImageJ to extract the G values. It disclosed no significant difference in the variance of the three approaches revealing their precision. Then the means of the three groups were statistically compared by the ANOVA test that confirmed no significant difference (Table 2 ). Similarly, the Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [35] were used to statistically compare the performance of Color Grab and RGB Detector software in measuring the H values. The obtained results confirmed no significant difference between the two software (Table 2). These outcomes ensure the reliability and comparability of the tested software offering great flexibility to the method.

To validate the even distribution of the excitation light and equal irradiation of all indicator zones in the paper-based device, 16 indicator areas were coated with GQDs (15 μL of 100 μg/mL solution), placed inside the imaging box, and its image was captured. Next, the images were processed by the RGB detector, Color Grab, and ImageJ software. The results are illustrated in Fig. 5 . The results showed that the mean green value (G0) obtained by the three software equals 253.5, 252.5, and 253.1 a.u with a standard deviation SD of 0.34, 0.52, and 0.44, respectively. The mean (H0) values obtained using RGB detector and Color Grab programs are 178.13 and 178.69 a.u with SD of 0.72 and 0.48, respectively. Furthermore, the average grayscale (Y0) is 253.12 a.u with SD of 0.32. These results revealed the reliability of the fluorescence intensity of all indicator zones. Thus confirming that all the indicator zones of the paper-based device are evenly excited and the captured images can be constantly processed regardless of the used software.

The gradual quenching of the blue fluorescence of the GQDs along with the increase of CZ concentration can be easily recognized by the naked eye under UV illumination (Fig. 4a) and quantified by the all-in-one device. The calibration curves are illustrated in (Fig. 4b-g). Good linearity was achieved using either the G, Y, or H values as the analytical signals indicating the suitability of these coordinates for the quantification of CZ (Table 1). The H values obtained by the RGB detector software have the best linearity with the highest r (0.998). Besides, working with H values is more user-friendly since it excludes prerequisite data curation before analysis where the H values are directly used in quantification in contrast to the G and Y values. Thus, we decided to employ this coordinate for the next studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and validation of the designed all-in-one device
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the developed all-in-one device was evaluated according to the ICH guideline for the validation of analytical procedures [36]. The linear regression equation, correlation coefficient (r), and dynamic linear range are summarized in Table 1.

The limits of quantification and detection, LOD and LOQ, respectively, were calculated according to the ICH guideline [36] as LOD = 3.3
σ a
and LOQ = 10
σ a
(where σ is the SD of the response and a is the slope of the calibration curve). The obtained results are 12 and 36 ng/zone, for LOD and LOQ respectively.

The intra-day precision of the method representing the repeatability over a short time interval was assessed by repeated analysis of three concentrations of CZ three consecutive times on the same day. The inter-day precision study was also conducted to investigate the different day variations by analysis of three concentrations of CZ on three successive days using three independent paper-based chips. The obtained values of the SD and relative standard deviations (RSD) indicate the high precision of the proposed method at both levels (Table 3 ).

Furthermore, the reproducibility of the paper-based chips has been tested for individual chips fabricated under identical optimum fabrication conditions. Three devices have been fabricated and separately used to analyze different concentrations of CZ. The results of the three experiments were statistically analyzed by the Brown-Forsythe test and ANOVA test [35]. The Brown-Forsythe test showed that the three tested chips have no significant difference in their variance confirming high precision (Table 4 ). Furthermore, the ANOVA test demonstrated a non-significant difference between the means of the three different paper-based chips (Table 4). Thus, the results of the statistical comparison of the performance of the three independent devices endorse the reproducibility and reliability of the paper-based chips.

The accuracy of the all-in-one device was validated by comparing the obtained results with the results of a reported HPLC-UV method [31]. Statistical comparison by Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test [35] shows no significant differences between the two methods (Table 5 ). This outcome confirms the accuracy, precision, and reliability of the new device.

The selectivity of the designed device for CZ was tested in the presence of some potentially co-existing substances such as glucose, glycine, starch, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Cl−, and SO4 2- at concentrations two orders of magnitude higher than the drug. Furthermore, the selectivity was verified in presence of some drugs from different pharmacological classes such as paracetamol, pseudoephedrine, etidronate disodium, famotidine, and acyclovir with concentrations about 10 times higher than that of CZ. As shown in Table 6 , the existence of the interfering substances does not have a substantial influence on the determination of CZ. The results revealed that the %change of H value caused by the interfering substances did not exceed 1.55%. This indicates the good selectivity of the method and its reliability and practicability for the determination of CZ in different matrices.

Herein, we optimized a conventional fluorimetry method as an analog of our all-in-one device using the benchtop apparatus with two main goals in mind: (i) to validate the all-in-one device as a reliable platform for the analysis of CZ and comparing the obtained results with that of a benchtop apparatus and (ii) to be utilized for the full elucidation of the underlying mechanism for turning-off the fluorescence of GQDs by CZ.

For achieving the most ideal conditions to determine CZ with the conventional fluorimetry approach, different experimental factors were inspected. The influence of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) has been explored over the range of 2.1–11.5 and it was found that the pH has no impact on the quenching efficiency, thus it was convenient and simpler to conduct this study without the addition of any buffer. Furthermore, the impact of GQDs final concentration was studied over the range of 0.2–0.6 μg/mL. We found that the variation of the concentration of GQDs did not change the quenching efficiency F0/F. As rationally expected, the increase in the concentration of GQDs is associated with a corresponding increase in its fluorescence intensity (F0). However, in the presence of a certain amount of the drug with variable concentrations of GQDs, the fluorescence intensities were attenuated by the same percentage leading to approximately constant F0/F values over the entire tested concentration range of GQDs. A final concentration of 0.5 μg/mL GQDs was selected as the optimum giving a high fluorescence intensity and a wide dynamic range. The performance of the sensing system was also evaluated in different diluting solvents like water, methanol, and ethanol. The quenching efficiency was not remarkably affected by changing the solvent, so that, water was the solvent of choice in this study for safety, simplicity, and economic reasons.

Under these conditions, the obtained calibration curve of Ln (F0/F) versus CZ concentration shows good linearity (r = 0.9993) over the concentration range of 0.42–84.6 μM (Fig. S2a, Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidation of the mechanism of sensing of CZ by the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of GQDs (λ ex/λ em of 350/440 nm) was efficiently quenched upon the addition of CZ (Fig. 6 a). Since there are many probable reasons for fluorescence quenching like ground state complexation, collisional quenching, inner filter effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [37], we performed a series of experiments using conventional fluorimetry to elucidate the mechanism of quenching.

CZ shows a UV-absorption peak that strongly overlaps with both excitation and emission spectra of GQDs (Fig. 6b), thus IFE and FRET mechanisms might be involved in the quenching process. IFE occurs when the quencher's absorption spectrum overlaps with the fluorophore's excitation or emission band or both, thus, the quencher absorbs such radiations. On the other hand, FRET may come to effect only when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the emission spectrum of the fluorophore allowing the energy transfer from the fluorophore to the quencher [37].

Considering the prospect of IFE, the observed fluorescence of GQDs in presence of increasing concentrations of CZ (Fobs) was corrected to exclude IFE according to the relationship given in Eq. (1) [38]:
1
F corr
=
F obs
x
a n t i
log
(
A
e x
+
A
e m
2
)
Where Fcorr is the corrected fluorescence intensity after subtraction of IFE, Aex, and Aem are the absorbance of the quencher (CZ) at excitation and emission wavelength of GQDs (350 and 440 nm, respectively).

Then, the efficiency of fluorescence suppression (%E) was calculated for both Fobs and Fcorr by applying Eq. (2):
2
% E
=
[ 1 −
(
F
F 0
)
]
× 100

A plot of %E versus the concentration of CZ [M] was plotted. The results displayed in Fig. 6c show that fluorescence quenching is attributed totally to the IFE mechanism since almost 100% of the suppressed efficiency is lost by removing the IFE.

In contrary to the dynamic and static quenching processes, the IFE is not affected by temperature whilst increasing the temperature causes enhancement of the dynamic quenching and weakening of the static quenching. Thus, to confirm the IFE, Stern-Volmer analysis of the quenching data (F0/F versus [CZ]) was conducted at 293, 303, and 313 K by applying the following equation [37]:
3 F0/F = 1 + K [Q]
Where K is the quenching constant and [Q] is the quencher concentration. It can be concluded from Fig. 6d that the relationship is linear, but the slopes and the quenching constants are not temperature-dependent (Table 7 ). This result confirmed the exclusion of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms.

Principally, IFE is a non-irradiation energy conversion process that takes place via diminution of the excitation light or absorption of the fluorescence radiation [38]. In such a process no new compound is formed since no chemical interaction takes place between the fluorophore and the quencher. Therefore, the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore would not change in the presence of a quencher [37]. As depicted in Fig. 6e, GQDs absorption peak recorded using water as a reference solution is identical to the absorption peak of GQDs-CZ using the same concentration of the drug in the reference solution. This evidence shows that no new substance is formed confirming that the quenching of GQDs is not a static process.

Since CZ is zwitterion with pka values of 11.64 and 1.98 [39], it is expected to show an overall neutral charge at pH 7.0. The %ionization of the acidic and basic groups of CZ was calculated to confirm this conclusion and found to be 99.9991 and 99.997%, respectively [40] indicating overall neutrality. This outweighs that neither static nor dynamic quenching are likely to participate in the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by CZ. The static quenching is based on ground state complexation that needs approaching of fluorophore-quencher pair and this requires electrostatic attraction, whereas, the dynamic quenching occurs via the collision of molecules that is assisted greatly by the electrostatic repulsive force. Since CZ molecules are neutral under experimental conditions, the two mechanisms are not supported and the IFE is the likely fluorescence quenching mechanism.

Another evidence that approves the IFE as the quenching mechanism, is the nature of fluorescence quencher's concentrations relationship observed in this study [38]. Plotting of F0/F versus CZ concentration showed exponential relationship over the concentration range of 0.42–84.6 μM, meanwhile, good linear correlation (r= 0.9937) could be achieved at lower concentrations within the range 0.42–19.0 μM (Fig. S2b, Supplementary Material). On the other hand, plotting Ln F0/F versus CZ concentration produces excellent linear correlation (r= 0.9993) over the entire concentration range of 0.42–84.6 μM (Fig. S2a, Supplementary Material). This specific behavior distinguishes the IFE from other quenching mechanisms [38]. A simplified illustration of the sensing mechanism via IFE is shown in Fig. 1d.

The dependence of the developed system on IFE-based sensing contributes greatly to its selectivity. For a compound to interfere with assays based on IFE, there should be a significant overlap between its absorption spectrum and the excitation and/or fluorescence spectra of the fluorophore (GQDs). Besides, it should have a strong light absorption and consequently a high molar absorption coefficient to exhibit a significant interference with the assay [38]. Thus, it is not easy to encounter significant interference from the common excipient and the tested interferent drugs. On the other hand, CZ is a strong UV-absorbing compound that shows strong overlapping with both the excitation and emission spectra of GQDs (Fig. 6b) with calculated molar absorption coefficient (ε) equals to 27.3 × 103 and 7.0 × 103 L/mol.cm at 350 and 440 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance of the designed all-in-one device and the conventional fluorimetry method
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure reliable results and verify the method relevancy, the results obtained by the conventional fluorimetry technique were statistically compared with those obtained by the all-in-one device by Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test. As demonstrated from the results shown in Table 8 , no significant difference between the two techniques was observed. Given the portability, miniaturization, small sample and reagent volumes, low cost, ease of usage, these results validate the reliability of the designed device for the determination of CZ and ensured comparable accuracy and precision with the benchtop apparatus.

The applicability of the integrated all-in-one device was evaluated for the determination of CZ in its pharmaceutical tablets to assess its practicability for real applications. To this end, three different concentrations from CZ tablets were analyzed by the all-in-one device and HPLC-UV [31] in parallel. As shown in Table 5, the average %recovery of CZ analyzed by the all-in-one is 98.5 with an SD of 0.8 while the average %recovery obtained by the HPLC-UV method is 97.4 with an SD of 0.5. Statistical comparison of the results of the two methods by the Student t-test and the Variance ratio F-test [35] showed a non-significant difference between the two methods regarding accuracy and precision (Table 5). This result confirmed the consistency between the designed all-in-one device and HPLC-UV [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of residual amounts of CZ on the equipment surface
ce:section.ce:para: The rapidness, sensitivity, selectivity, and high throughput of CZ determination by the designed all-in-one device encouraged the authors to further study its applicability for the determination of CZ residuals on the surfaces of manufacturing equipment. The elimination of drugs’ residuals from the equipment utilized for manufacturing purposes is an essential step for good manufacturing practice to avoid cross-contamination of pharmaceutical products.

Computational toxicological evaluation of CZ via ADMET prediction using admetSAR (version 2.0) software [41] showed some characteristics that may have a role in the drug's safety profile and its risk to a patient if he is exposed to traces by cross-contamination of next batches of other pharmaceutical compounds during the manufacturing process. ADMET prediction revealed the likely hepatotoxicity of CZ. Furthermore, it is classified as a toxic compound by the Tetrahymena pyriformis toxicity model. The built-in genotoxicity model based on a large database of in vivo micronucleus assay results revealed the potential genotoxicity of CZ. The ADMET prediction showed also that CZ is an inhibitor of the hepatic transport proteins organic anion-transporting polypeptide OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, thus it can cause clinically relevant drug-drug interactions especially when residuals of CZ remained on the equipment surface after manufacturing and cross-contaminates the next formulation of another drug. These results explain the need for an analytical method for residual determination of CZ on the instrument surface to validate the cleaning procedure [42]. Another factor that urges conducting this study is the poor solubility of CZ in water and subsequent difficult cleaning of equipment [43]. Reported literature lacks an analytical method that is related to residual determination to validate the cleaning procedure. This encouraged the authors to apply the designed all-in-one device for trace level determination of CZ on a stainless steel surface to ensure cleaning efficiency.

The FDA [44] did not set acceptance specifications for the cleaning process. However, the FDA guide to inspections validation of cleaning processes recommended testing the analytical method and the sampling method used to confirm that residuals can be recovered from the equipment surface and to determine the recovery%. Thus, we intended to test the developed all-in-one device for this goal.

We used the swab sampling technique that is preferred by the FDA [44] and USP [45] since it is suitable for poorly soluble contaminants, such as CZ, and hardly accessed areas. In this investigation, we adopted the 10 ppm acceptance limit that is based on the analytical detection level since it is widely applied by several pharmaceutical firms [44].

The recovery of the extraction process is validated by spiking CZ on a stainless-steel plate surface at known concentrations to determine the recovery where a recovery >80% is considered good, > 50% is reasonable while <50% is disputed. The recovery factor was calculated as follows [46]:
4
%Recovery by the swab =
Calculated test result x 100
Known spiked concentration

The concentration of CZ was compared with the spiked concentration at three concentration levels, three replicate each (Table 9 ). The obtained results revealed %recoveries within the range of 96.4–99.4% with RSD% ranged from 0.06 to 0.24%. The good %recoveries with small RSD% demonstrate the validity and reliability of the all-in-one device and the sampling process for residual determination of CZ on the surface of the equipment and recommend the method for further validation by the manufacturing firms for inspections validation of cleaning processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method greenness and implementation of sustainability concept in the analytical laboratories
ce:section.ce:para: The harmony of the newly designed device with the twelve rubrics of green analytical chemistry was valued utilizing the new analytical greenness metric and software: AGREE [47]. This tool considers the pretreatment, size, and throughput of the sample, the location of the analytical apparatus, integration of the analytical procedure, demands of energy and reagents, automation and miniaturization, derivatization, production and treatment of waste, origin and toxicity of reagents, and protection of analyst. The software uses a scoring system that translates the twelve rubrics into digits and gives a mark within the range of 0–1, where 1 means an ideally green method that met all the criteria. Evaluation of the developed technique by the AGREE metric revealed excellent greenness with a mark of 0.87 (Fig. 7 a). The potential of the application of the developed all-in-one device as a substitute to the large in-position instruments in the implementation of the sustainability concept in analytical laboratories is very noticeable (Fig. 7b). The minimal usage of hazardous chemicals, negligible waste production, minimum energy consumption, and low cost of the fabricated device are vital aspects that contribute to the greenness of the technique. Furthermore, miniaturization and integration of the analytical system is a very prominent characteristic that distinguishes the designed device. The portability and the lightweight of the all-in-one device enabled at-line analysis that reduces the time delay in attaining the analytical information. Furthermore, all the elements for the assembly of the device are simple and available, and its construction can be realized at a cost of about 20 $/set which verifies the cost-effectiveness of the design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: All-in-one device critical comparison towards established literature
ce:section.ce:para: A critical comparison of the performance of the newly fabricated all-in-one device and the published techniques for CZ determination including LC/MS, HPLC, TLC-densitometry, and conventional fluorimetry is presented in Table 10 . The lowest LOD was achieved by the LC/MS methods [29,30] followed by the all-in-one device that surpasses all of the other techniques. The all-in-one device is fully portable and can be used at the point of need in contrast to all of the reported methods that are based on benchtop large instruments. Another distinctive feature of the all-in-one device is the minimum experience required to operate it since the analytical system is greatly integrated and the smartphone gives a reliable readout by simple steps. An additional unique characteristic of the all-in-one device is the high sample throughput since 16 samples were analyzed/single run. The number of samples can be further increased by increasing the number of sensing zones/paper-based chips. This allows the analysis of many samples in the shortest time. Considering the costs of the analysis, the all-in-one device is the cheapest method compared to methods utilizing LC-MS, LC, TLC, or fluorimetry. Generally, the chromatography techniques consume plenty of costly organic solvents [12]. Furthermore, these analytical techniques need experience to operate the devices. Overall, the advantages of the all-in-one device are manifest emphasizing its potential for different applications.

The innovative use of correction pen to create confining barriers on the paper substrate in this method offers many advantages over the techniques reported for this purpose, such as photolithography, wax printing, screen-printing, flexography, and laser treating [3,48]. Using the correction pen for patterning of the paper substrate is user-friendly, does not need any special training or skills, and excludes the use of any devices or distinct treatments. Besides, it is a very cheap technique that is available and accessible to researchers and developers worldwide, especially in low-income laboratories. Moreover, the correction fluid has been proven as a hydrophobic/lipophobic material that confines both aqueous and organic solvents very well. Consequently, the developed technique introduces a promising platform for further applications in sensing and diagnostics. On the other hand, the device and photoresists used for the creation of hydrophobic barriers in photolithography, e.g., octadecyltrichlorosilane and poly(o-nitrobenzylmethacrylate), are expensive. The printers and materials used for wax printing technique are also costly despite offering a rapid fabrication process. On the other hand, the screen-printing method is limited by mandating different screens for different patterns of the paper-based analytical devices. Besides, the paper-based analytical devices fabricated by the laser treating technique are not suited for storage which limits its applications. Meanwhile, flexographic printing requires different printing plates and two prints of polystyrene [48]. It is noteworthy to highlight that the materials usually used to create hydrophobic barriers, e.g., wax, polymers, silicone, and methylsilsequioxanes, cannot properly restrict organic solvents [15] which limits their applications and gives an extra advantage and importance to the developed technique employing correction fluid.

The quantitative determination of the anti-hemorrhagic drug CZ has been successfully achieved with a new all-in-one device based on a smartphone and GQDs-modified paper-based chip. The all-in-one device is composed of a black carton box and a UV 365 nm flashlight that emits the excitation light to uniformly irradiate the paper-based chip. Fluorescent GQDs have been coated on a paper-based chip designed using a simple and convenient technique for applying hydrophobic/lipophobic barriers using a correction pen. CZ quenches the fluorescence of GQDs by IFE. The smartphone has been used for taking images for the paper-based chips and analyzing them by free software to obtain the analytical signal as the Hue coordinate. The all-in-one device features many distinctive advantages including the high sensing ability towards CZ with high sensitivity and reliability. Furthermore, the device is fully portable, user-friendly, easily used, and cheap with a total cost not exceeding 20 $. The device is characterized also by high sample throughput where 16 samples have been determined/run with a possibility for increasing the number of samples as needed. This smartphone-based all-in-one device is readily suited for application in analytical chemistry laboratories in low-income countries for vast purposes such as drug determination, water analysis, food safety, and point-of-need detection.
The authors received no specific funding for this work.
